query: Vipeholm study findings on sugar restriction and poor hygiene conditions

retrieved_docs.title: Diet and Nutrition to Prevent Dental Problems -- Function -- The Amount and Frequency of Sugar Consumed
retrieved_docs.content: Several studies evaluate the association between the amount and frequency of sugar intake and dental caries. For example, one study found that 3-year-old children that ingested sugars four to five times per day were six times more likely to have a high caries rate than children with the lowest frequency of sugar intake. [4] The effects of sugar intake on caries activity were studied in 436 patients from a mental health facility over five years. Among other discoveries, they concluded that if sugar was consumed with meals no more than four times per day, it had a low impact on caries development. [10] By contrast, increased sugar consumption between meals was linked to high caries activity. [10] The “sugar-caries relationship” also depends on oral hygiene, fluoride exposure, and socio-economic status. [4]

retrieved_docs.content: Limiting added sugar (sucrose) and refined carbohydrates is critical in improving food quality and will generally reach a moderate carbohydrate (<45% carbohydrates) level.

retrieved_docs.title: Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: BRFSS prevalence data are published annually in the CDC's Morbidity and Mortality Weekly Report, an epidemiological report used to disseminate recommendations and various public health information reported to the CDC by each state health department. [11] The BFRSS has recently been used to study the following: Cost-related medication nonadherence [13] [14] [15] Trends in vaccination rates [16] Examine the effects of Medicaid expansion [17] Assess the impact of COVID-19 on alcohol sales [18] Assess the usage of sugar-sweetened beverages in women [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Epidemiologic and Etiologic Considerations of Obesity -- Issues of Concern -- Prenatal and Childhood Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Another area for improvement is by adding healthier school lunch options. Results from a study using nonlinear regression models found that children who attended public schools had a higher BMI regardless of socioeconomic status. Furthermore, those children eligible for free or reduced-cost lunch and breakfast had a higher BMI. [39] Even though additional data is required to determine the impact of limiting the availability of nutrient-poor or high-sugar goods in schools on obesity, some study results have shown a net-positive result. [40] [41] [42]

retrieved_docs.title: Glucose Tolerance Test -- Interfering Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Several precautions must be observed when preparing for and performing the OGTT. The assessment should not be conducted in patients with an infection, traumatic injuries, or severe illness. Drugs such as corticosteroids and diuretics, which may impair glucose tolerance, must be discontinued before the test if possible. The patient should have followed an unrestricted diet containing at least 150 g of carbohydrates daily for at least 3 days and avoided unusual or vigorous exercise during that period.

retrieved_docs.title: Behavior Modification for Lifestyle Improvement -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Many health-related behaviors have been described in the medical literature. [13] However, a large part of the literature has focused on a small number of categories that have shown to be strongly associated with health outcomes, including diet quality, body weight, physical activity, smoking avoidance/cessation, and limiting alcohol consumption. [14] For this reason, interventions prioritizing behavior modification in these categories will likely substantially impact health outcomes associated with most chronic non-communicable diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: Epidemiologic and Etiologic Considerations of Obesity -- Issues of Concern -- Medical Factors of Obesity
retrieved_docs.content: The use of intensive insulin therapy and home glucose monitoring often manages diabetes. Tight control of blood glucose is critical in preventing the vascular complications of the condition. However, insulin therapy itself is associated with weight gain. [54] [55] Some studies have suggested that metformin may result in weight loss in patients with type 2 diabetes. However, the evidence to establish this association is limited. [56]

retrieved_docs.title: Early Childhood Caries -- Etiology -- Socioeconomic and Ethnic Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Early childhood caries is most common in children from a low socioeconomic background: prenatal and perinatal malnutrition predisposes to enamel hypoplasia; [17] [18] [9] [19] [20] a lower level of education and literacy correlates with poor oral hygiene and sugary food consumption; lack of access to dental care; and lack of fluoride exposure.

retrieved_docs.title: Carbuncle -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Predisposing conditions that lead to a compromised or broken down skin barrier include eczema, diabetes mellitus, alcohol use disorder, malnutrition, immunodeficiency, obesity, and poor hygiene. [2] [6] [13] Other predisposing factors include chronic methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) colonization, living in crowded conditions, hyperhidrosis, and anemia. [5] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Diet and Nutrition to Prevent Dental Problems -- Function -- Sugar Alcohols
retrieved_docs.content: Sugar alcohols result from reducing the carbonyl group of sugars. [11] They are non-fermentable and heat resistant. [11] They do not induce insulin secretion, trigger a rapid glucose rise in the blood, or increase lipoprotein-lipase activity. [11] They are suitable sweeteners for patients with diabetes mellitus. [11] They are not fermented or only a little by oral microorganisms; therefore, they are non-cariogenic. [11] Sugar alcohols may even promote remineralization of the enamel. [11]

Health-compromising behaviors include physical inactivity, substance abuse, and chronic excess calorie intake. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Prevention Strategies -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Populations in low socioeconomic status bear a disproportionate burden of disease and often lack access to preventive care. [12] This disparity highlights the necessity of prioritizing public health interventions, particularly in historically underserved communities. Studies consistently demonstrate an inverse relationship between socioeconomic status and healthy behaviors, such as smoking cessation, physical activity, and diet quality, underscoring ongoing health inequities driven by socioeconomic factors.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2479
retrieved_docs.content: Disease condition determination, uncontrolled is associated with diabetes mellitus with complications, indicating that an uncontrolled disease state is linked to the presence of diabetes mellitus that has progressed to a complicated form, underscoring the importance of effective disease management to prevent or mitigate diabetic complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION represent a collective group of perinatal problems that encompass short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. Short gestation, defined as delivery before full term, often results in low birth weight because the infant has had insufficient time for normal growth. Low birth weight, in turn, frequently reflects fetal growth retardation, a condition in which the fetus does not achieve its genetically expected size. These three entities are closely interrelated, each contributing to the broader spectrum of fetal growth and malnutrition disorders, and together they increase the risk of adverse neonatal outcomes. Effective maternal care that optimizes nutrition and prevents premature delivery is essential for reducing the incidence of these interconnected conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2614
retrieved_docs.content: Disease condition determination, uncontrolled is described as a general clinical state that is interpreted by clinicians (is_interpreted_by) and is associated with diabetes mellitus with complications. This indicates that an uncontrolled disease condition determination reflects a concerning overall clinical status and frequently co‑occurs with complicated diabetes mellitus, highlighting the need for careful assessment and targeted management in patients presenting with such uncontrolled conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2306
retrieved_docs.content: COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY is a subcategory within the broader group of Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium. Among the many obstetric complications encompassed by this larger category, diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium represents a specific type of complication. This relationship underscores that gestational diabetes and related disorders of glucose metabolism are recognized as important pregnancy‑related complications and are included in the overall spectrum of conditions that can affect maternal and fetal outcomes during pregnancy, delivery, and the postpartum period. Effective identification and management of these glucose‑related complications are essential components of comprehensive obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2041
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin K 2 and vitamin K 3 are both classified as vitamins. As members of the vitamin group, they are directly related to نقص التغذية (nutritional deficiency), highlighting that insufficient intake or absorption of these vitamins can contribute to a state of nutritional deficiency. Recognizing vitamin K 2 and vitamin K 3 within the broader context of vitamins is essential for identifying and preventing nutrient‑related health problems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY is classified as a subcategory within the broader group of Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium, indicating that these health issues are primarily associated with the gestational period and its immediate aftermath.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2372
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance that arises during pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium is recognized as a specific type of complication within the broader category of complications of pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium. This condition represents a distinct obstetric complication where altered glucose metabolism adversely affects maternal and fetal health, underscoring the importance of monitoring and managing glucose levels throughout gestation, delivery, and the postpartum period.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2434
retrieved_docs.content: Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium is a broad category that encompasses specific maternal health problems such as hypertension and/or vomiting complicating pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium, as well as diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance occurring during pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium. Both hypertension‑related vomiting and gestational diabetes represent distinct subtypes of the overall complications associated with the perinatal period, highlighting the need for careful monitoring and targeted management of these conditions to protect maternal and fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2023
retrieved_docs.content: Nutritional deficiencies, referred to in Arabic as نقص التغذية, represent a parent category that encompasses a wide range of specific deficiency conditions. This overarching concept includes various forms of inadequate intake or absorption of essential nutrients and serves as the broader classification under which individual deficiency disorders are organized. Recognizing nutritional deficiencies as the parent concept helps clinicians systematically assess and address the diverse manifestations of micronutrient and macronutrient insufficiencies in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2460
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes (مرض السكري) includes specific forms such as DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), which is mapped to the broader category of diabetes, and the condition described as “diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy; childbirth; or the puerperium,” which is an inverse subclass of diabetes. Clinically, diabetes is associated with retinal disorder (اضطراب في الشبكية), highlighting its potential impact on ocular health. Moreover, diabetes is linked to various risk factors (rizikové faktory), emphasizing the need to identify and manage these contributors to improve patient outcomes.
